* feat(jump_rr): save t-statistic matrices for perturbation effect-size ranking

Build toward a new "perturbation ranking" table — the inverse of the
existing Feature Ranking table. Instead of "which features are significant
for perturbation X?", the new table answers "which perturbations have the
strongest effect on feature X?", ranked by |t-statistic| descending.

Why t-statistics instead of p-values:
The existing compound_significance_full.parquet contains BH-corrected
p-values rounded to 5 decimals (da.around(featstat, 5)). This creates
massive ties: 99.9% of top-50-per-feature rows have p=0.0, making
p-value ranking effectively alphabetical by JCP2022 ID. T-statistics
capture direction and magnitude without this rounding problem.

Code changes:
- significance.py: pvals_from_profile() now returns (corrected_p_values,
  t_statistics) instead of just corrected_p_values. The t-stats were
  already computed internally but discarded; they are now returned.
- calculate_features.py: unpacks the new tuple return and saves
  {dset_type}_tstat_full.parquet alongside the existing significance
  matrix — same shape (n_compounds x n_features) with Metadata_JCP2022.
- tests/test_t.py: updated to unpack the tuple from pvals_from_profile.
- uv.lock: pytest moved from dev to test dependency group.
- .gitignore: added for local dev artifacts.
- CLAUDE.md: updated install command and added data exploration notes.

Design doc (docs/plans/) captures the full investigation: existing table
analysis, Gene Rank misnomer, 999999 sentinel values, p-value tie
problem, SQL query template for generating the final table from the
t-stat matrix via DuckDB UNPIVOT.

Next steps (require GPU re-run):
- Re-run pipeline to generate compound_tstat_full.parquet
- Generate compound_perturbation_ranking.parquet via DuckDB SQL
- Upload to S3 / Zenodo

* fix(jump_rr): preserve both ranks for overlap cells in Polars merge

When we switched rank unification from DuckDB to Polars, we started
combining per-perturbation (`df_x`) and per-feature (`df_y`) selections via
`pl.concat(..., how="diagonal")` and then grouping by `(x, y)`.

The aggregation used:
- `pl.col("rankf").first()`
- `pl.col("rankg").first()`

For cells present in both selections, each group contains two rows:
1) row from `df_x` with `rankf` set and `rankg = null`
2) row from `df_y` with `rankg` set and `rankf = null`

`first()` is evaluated independently per column and may pick the row where the
column is null, which can drop one of the ranks on overlap cells. That breaks
the intended behavior documented in the code comments: overlap cells should
retain both ranks.

Fix:
- aggregate with first non-null value per rank column:
  - `pl.col("rankf").drop_nulls().first().alias("rankf")`
  - `pl.col("rankg").drop_nulls().first().alias("rankg")`

This keeps existing behavior for non-overlap cells and restores correct output
for overlap cells.

Validation:
- verified with a minimal Polars repro that overlap groups now return both
  `rankf` and `rankg` instead of one side becoming null.

Key info (date, location, speakers) is typically at the top of the
page, so 1500 chars is sufficient. Added payload size logging.

Fix: defer ComposeView addition until after parent chain has owners

ComposeView resolves ViewTreeLifecycleOwner in its own
onAttachedToWindow. Previous approach added ComposeView to the
container before attachment, so both attached simultaneously and
the parent chain wasn't ready yet. Now use post{} to add
ComposeView after the FrameLayout's onAttachedToWindow has
stamped owners on all ancestors.

Also fix fold/unfold: force recreate input view on config change.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>
